meta.title: Planet Her: Doja Cat's Cosmic Pop Empire
meta.subtitle: A Review of Doja Cat's Third Studio Album
meta.author: Jenny Batalla

albumDescription.label: Kemosabe and RCA Records
albumDescription.summary: "Planet Her" is Doja Cat's third studio album, released on June 25, 2021, through Kemosabe and RCA Records. The 14-track project positions Doja Cat as the ruler of a fictional celestial body, using this sci-fi concept to explore themes of femininity, power, desire, and emotional vulnerability. The album features high-profile collaborations with SZA, Ariana Grande, The Weeknd, Young Thug, and JID, showcasing Doja's ability to hold her own alongside pop and hip-hop royalty. Produced primarily by Y2K, Yeti Beats, and Tizhimself, "Planet Her" blends pop, R&B, hip-hop, disco-funk, and hyperpop into a cohesive yet genre-fluid soundscape. The album debuted at number two on the Billboard 200, was certified platinum by the RIAA, and earned three Grammy nominations including Album of the Year, solidifying Doja Cat's transition from viral internet sensation to legitimate pop powerhouse.

body: When Doja Cat declared herself ruler of a fictional planet with her third studio album, she wasn't being hyperbolic—she was making a mission statement. "Planet Her," released in June 2021, is a 14-track journey through a meticulously constructed pop universe where genre boundaries dissolve, where Nicki Minaj and Ariana Grande orbit the same sonic galaxy, and where Doja Cat reigns as the architect of her own gleaming, genre-fluid empire.

The album opens with "Woman," a swaggering declaration of feminine power draped in disco-funk production that recalls the glittering dancefloors of Studio 54 reimagined for the TikTok generation. The bass line prowls like a panther in heels while Doja's vocals glide effortlessly between breathy coos and full-throated belting. It's a thesis statement: this is pop music as shape-shifting spectacle, refusing to be pinned down or categorized. The production—handled primarily by Y2K and Yeti Beats—layers live instrumentation with synthetic textures, creating what feels less like a song and more like a three-dimensional sonic hologram.

What makes "Planet Her" particularly fascinating is Doja Cat's willingness to let her collaborators shine without surrendering her artistic vision. "Kiss Me More," featuring SZA, became the album's commercial juggernaut, and for good reason. The chemistry between the two artists crackles with playful sensuality, their voices intertwining over production that samples Olivia Newton-John's "Physical" while sounding utterly contemporary. The song captures what DiYanni describes as successful music writing's challenge: it translates "fleeting, evanescent sounds" into something tangible and memorable. Here, Doja and SZA achieve that rare pop alchemy where catchiness doesn't compromise complexity.

The album's sonic palette is deliberately eclectic, almost defiantly so. "Need to Know" pulses with hyperpop aggression, its distorted vocals and apocalyptic synths suggesting what might happen if Charli XCX and Britney Spears had a musical offspring raised on internet culture. "You Right," featuring The Weeknd, slows the tempo into sultry R&B territory, all shadows and whispered confessions. The production here is minimalist and claustrophobic in the best way, creating an intimate atmosphere that contrasts sharply with the album's more bombastic moments.

But "Planet Her" isn't just a showcase for genre tourism—it's a statement about contemporary pop's relationship with identity and authenticity. Doja Cat has faced criticism throughout her career for alleged inauthenticity, for being too online, too willing to play different roles. "Planet Her" responds by leaning into that multiplicity, suggesting that in 2021, versatility isn't a weakness but a superpower. "Get Into It (Yuh)" samples Nicki Minaj's "Super Bass" while building something entirely new, a meta-commentary on influence and homage in the streaming era.

The album's middle section occasionally sags under its own ambitions. "Love to Dream" and "Alone" feel like necessary breathing room but lack the hooks that make the surrounding tracks so immediately grabbing. They're atmospheric interludes on an album that thrives on maximalist pop spectacle. This is where we see the tension DiYanni identifies in music writing—the challenge of balancing subjective response with objective analysis. These tracks may not showcase Doja's strengths, but they serve a structural purpose, providing dynamic contrast.

"Ain't Shit," featuring JID, injects hip-hop braggadocio into the proceedings, with Doja rapping with the kind of technical precision that reminds listeners she's not just a pop star playing at rap—she's genuinely skilled at both. JID's verse provides the perfect complement, his rapid-fire delivery matching Doja's own verbal dexterity. The production here is sparse and percussive, letting the wordplay take center stage.

The album closes with "Naked," a vulnerable moment that strips away the cosmic production and futuristic sheen. Over a simple guitar line, Doja sings about insecurity and desire with a rawness that feels earned after the album's journey through various pop personas. It's a reminder that underneath the sci-fi concept and the genre-hopping, there's an actual artist making actual emotional connections.

From a production standpoint, "Planet Her" is immaculate—almost too immaculate. Every sound has been polished to a chrome shine, every vocal run Auto-Tuned to mathematical precision. This aesthetic fits the album's futuristic concept, but it occasionally creates an emotional distance. When Doja sings about vulnerability, the pristine production sometimes works against the message, creating a barrier between performer and listener.

Yet this tension is perhaps intentional. "Planet Her" exists in the uncanny valley between authenticity and performance, between human emotion and digital perfection. Doja Cat seems to be asking: in an era where we curate our identities online, where we perform versions of ourselves across social media platforms, what does authenticity even mean? The album doesn't answer this question so much as dance around it, which may be the most honest response possible.

The album's commercial success—it debuted at number two on the Billboard 200 and spawned multiple hit singles—suggests that Doja Cat's vision resonates with a generation that sees no contradiction in being simultaneously sincere and ironic, vulnerable and invincible, human and avatar. "Planet Her" isn't just an album; it's a manifesto for pop music in the age of digital multiplicity.

sections.heading: Conclusion
body: In the taxonomy of 2021 pop albums, "Planet Her" stands out for its refusal to choose a lane. It's R&B, it's rap, it's dance-pop, it's all of these and none of these simultaneously. Doja Cat has created her own planet, governed by her own rules, where the only law is that there are no laws—except, perhaps, the mandate to be endlessly, fearlessly inventive. Whether that planet has staying power beyond this particular cultural moment remains to be seen, but as a snapshot of pop music's current possibilities, "Planet Her" is a fascinating, if occasionally frustrating, testament to the genre's shape-shifting future.

topTracks.title: Kiss Me More
topTracks.description: The album's lead single and biggest commercial success, this infectious collaboration samples Olivia Newton-John's "Physical" while creating something entirely contemporary. The chemistry between Doja and SZA is palpable, their playful vocal interplay floating over bouncy production that feels simultaneously retro and futuristic. The song peaked at number three on the Billboard Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best Pop Duo/Group Performance.

topTracks.title: Woman
topTracks.description: A disco-funk anthem that channels '70s glamour through a modern lens. The song's confident lyrics celebrating femininity are matched by swaggering production featuring live bass, shimmering strings, and Doja's most dynamic vocal performance on the album. It's a statement of intent that sets the tone for everything that follows.

funFacts: Born Amala Ratna Zandile Dlamini, Doja Cat's father is South African actor Dumisani Dlamini, who appeared in the original Broadway production of "Sarafina!" Her stage name "Doja" comes from her love of marijuana and her favorite strain at the time.

At the 2022 Grammy Awards, "Kiss Me More" won Best Pop Duo/Group Performance. In her acceptance speech, she nearly missed receiving the award because she was in the bathroom, leading to a memorable moment when she rushed on stage.
